flashcards.front: What is a schedule in DBMS?
flashcards.back: A schedule is a chronological sequence of operations (read/write) from a set of concurrent transactions executing in a system.

flashcards.front: Define recoverability in the context of schedules.
flashcards.back: Recoverability refers to a schedule's ability to ensure that the database can be restored to a consistent state after a transaction failure without affecting already committed transactions.
A schedule is recoverable if no transaction commits until all transactions whose writes it has read have either committed or aborted.
A schedule is cascadeless (or avoiding cascading aborts) if a transaction only reads values written by committed transactions, so abort of one transaction doesn't cause others to abort.
A strict schedule ensures that no transaction reads or writes a data item written by an uncommitted transaction, providing the highest level of recoverability.
A dirty read occurs when a transaction reads data written by an uncommitted concurrent transaction, which may later be rolled back.
A serial schedule is one where transactions execute one after another without any interleaving of operations; no two transactions run concurrently.
Serializability is the property of a schedule producing the same result as some serial execution of the same transactions.
Conflict serializability exists when a schedule can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations (read-read operations are non-conflicting).
View serializability checks if a schedule is view-equivalent to a serial schedule, considering initial read, read-from, and final write relationships.
Two schedules are conflict-equivalent if they contain the same operations and order of conflicting operations (read-write or write-read or write-write) is the same.
Cascadeless rollback ensures that when a transaction aborts, no other transaction needs to be rolled back because no uncommitted data was read.

flashcards.front: State the hierarchy of schedule types.
flashcards.back: Strict Schedule ⊂ Cascadeless Schedule ⊂ Recoverable Schedule. Every strict schedule is cascadeless, and every cascadeless schedule is recoverable.
Conflict operations are operations from different transactions on the same data item where at least one is a write operation: read-write, write-read, or write-write conflicts.
Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single indivisible unit - either all its operations complete or none of them do.
